coredata.dc:title: Enhancing broadband absorption and photocurrent generation in carbon dots via P3HT integration
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gopi, Pavithra
Ponnusamy, Kathirvel
coredata.dc:description: The growing interest in carbon dots (CDs) arises from their diverse applications and unique properties. This study addresses challenges in CDs for photodetector (PD) applications, specifically surface defects and trap states hindering efficient charge transport. CDs/P3HT composites were prepared to overcome these issues by incorporating CDs in a poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) matrix. Broad absorption in spectroscopic characterization revealed its utility in fabricating a broadband PD. The CDs/P3HT PD displays a remarkable broadband photoresponse, spanning both UV and visible regions. The CDs and P3HT are effectively combined via non-covalent π-π interactions constituted by their conjugated systems. The π-π interaction increases electron delocalization and facilitates efficient charge transfer due to band alignment at the junction interface. Hence, fabricated CDs/P3HT PD demonstrated enhanced photocurrent compared to pure CDs, exhibiting high responsivity of 6.12 × 10−3 AW−1 and detectivity of 0.69 × 109 Jones. This study highlights the potential of CD/P3HT composites for broadband photodetector applications with enhanced photoelectric conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CDs/P3HT composite
Broadband photodetector
Microwave synthesis
Charge transport layer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, CDs/P3HT composite and fabrication of PD

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their sincere gratitude to Mr. K. Karthick and Mr. S. Praisudan from the GRD Centre for Materials Research, PSG College of Technology, for their invaluable contributions, insightful discussions, and feedback during this research. Additionally, the authors acknowledge Mr. G. Manikandan from the Spintronics and Functional Materials Laboratory, PSG College of Technology, for his assistance with the FTIR study. Special thanks to the PSG Institute of Advanced Studies for their generous support in facilitating HRTEM, XRD, PL, and Raman studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a, b) HRTEM image, c) Size distribution histogram, d) XRD pattern, e) Raman spectrum and f) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs, High resolution spectrum of b) C1s, c) O1s and d) N1s.

(a) FTIR spectra of P3HT and CDs/P3HT, (b) UV–Visible spectrum of CDs with Tauc plot (inset), (c) PL spectra of CDs, (d) UV–Visible spectra of P3HT and CDs/P3HT with Tauc plot (inset), and (e) PL spectra of P3HT and CDs/P3HT.

a) I–V curve of CDs PD, b) I–V curve of P3HT, c) I–V curve of CDs/P3HT PD, d) Responsivity and detectivity of CDs/P3HT PD with varying concentrations, e) Switching characteristics of CDs/P3HT PD at 3V.
Schematic of the synthesis of CDs, preparation of CDs/P3HT composite and structure of CDs/P3HT PD.
Photodetector parameters of CDs/P3HT with varying weight percentages under different illuminations.
A comparison of the photoresponse from previously published studies with our results.

head.ce:title: Enhancing broadband absorption and photocurrent generation in carbon dots via P3HT integration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: GRD Centre for Materials Research, Department of Physics, PSG College of Technology, Coimbatore, 641004, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: GRD Centre for Materials Research, Department of Physics, PSG College of Technology, Coimbatore, 641004, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing interest in carbon dots (CDs) arises from their diverse applications and unique properties. This study addresses challenges in CDs for photodetector (PD) applications, specifically surface defects and trap states hindering efficient charge transport. CDs/P3HT composites were prepared to overcome these issues by incorporating CDs in a poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) matrix. Broad absorption in spectroscopic characterization revealed its utility in fabricating a broadband PD. The CDs/P3HT PD displays a remarkable broadband photoresponse, spanning both UV and visible regions. The CDs and P3HT are effectively combined via non-covalent π-π interactions constituted by their conjugated systems. The π-π interaction increases electron delocalization and facilitates efficient charge transfer due to band alignment at the junction interface. Hence, fabricated CDs/P3HT PD demonstrated enhanced photocurrent compared to pure CDs, exhibiting high responsivity of 6.12 × 10−3 AW−1 and detectivity of 0.69 × 109 Jones. This study highlights the potential of CD/P3HT composites for broadband photodetector applications with enhanced photoelectric conversion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are zero-dimensional material with distinctive sp2/sp3 hybridized core at their centre, enveloped by functional groups whose composition varies based on the precursor materials used during synthesis. CDs have garnered pervasive interest stemming from their unique properties and versatile applications in recent years [1,2]. CDs exhibit strong UV absorption, making them valuable as active materials in UV PD applications [3,4]. However, despite their promising attributes, CDs still pose certain challenges that need to be addressed. CDs feature surface defects resulting from imperfect sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons, along with functional groups bearing dangling bonds and oxygen-containing moieties [5]. These defects can lead to the creation of trap states within CDs, which temporarily confine photogenerated electrons and holes, hindering their flow towards the electrodes. To overcome these limitations, researchers have explored the hybrid structures involving CDs [6–8]. Sahatiya et al. [9] introduced a flexible hybrid photodetector by combining two-dimensional molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) with zero-dimensional CDs. The Fermi level mismatch between CDs and MoS2 resulted in the formation of unipolar junctions, generating local electric fields that greatly enhanced charge separation efficiency under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light.

Furthermore, the photocurrent of pure CDs can be adversely affected by exposure to atmospheric air. CDs have a tendency to absorb oxygen from the air, leading to the formation of trap states [10]. Qing Zhang et al. [11] observed that the photocurrent of CDs increases under vacuum conditions compared to ambient air. Incorporating CDs into a polymer matrix can mitigate these issues, improving their stability and preventing agglomeration. Functional groups like hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs enable better anchoring of polymers [12]. This composite material of CDs and polymers has found applications in various fields, including biosensing, bioimaging, luminescence, electrochemical devices, and photodetectors [13–15]. The preparation of CDs/polymer composites involves several methods, including blending polymers with CDs, in situ synthesis of polymers in the presence of CDs, and one-step synthesis of CDs/polymer composites [16]. Although in situ synthesis and one-step synthesis methods are cost-effective and simple, they have shortcomings such as poor size control and long reaction times [17]. Vandana et al. [14] synthesized GQD using the hydrothermal method by heating for 3 h and prepared the PPy-GQD composite through a chemical oxidation polymerization process for 24 h to fabricate PD. Conversely, blending is a simpler method wherein CDs are conjugated with the polymer through high dispersion, especially suitable for large-scale production of composites [16]. Therefore, we opt for blending polymer with CDs to obtain well-dispersed polymer composites.

In our research, we have employed P3HT, a conducting polymer renowned for its remarkable electrical and optical properties which exhibit semi crystalline behaviour and broad absorption across the visible region [18–21]. Recent studies have extensively investigated P3HT as a charge transport layer when combined with various carbon nanomaterials, reporting enhanced charge transport and excellent stability [22]. Specifically, CDs with P3HT are widely studied in organic solar cells (OSC) to improve conversion efficiency through increased electron transfer [23–26]. P3HT is studied as an electron donor and CDs as an acceptor material in organic solar cells, which improve charge separation [7,26]. Cui et al. [26] reported that the graphitic structured blue greenish C-CQDs synthesized using CVD method increased the efficiency of P3HT:PC61BM solar cells by 11 %. Mei et al. [24] designed nanohybrids with P3HT using CQD and GQD and found that crab like supramolecules where the GQD attached with long chain P3HT gave improved charge density and mobility in OSC. Thus, it is believed that P3HT could improve the photoconductivity of CDs PD. Theoretical studies have also reported that incorporating GQDs into P3HT enhances charge transfer and conductivity through donor-acceptor interactions, highlighting their potential for optoelectronic applications [27]. The noncovalent interaction between them will reduce the defect states at the heterojunctions and improve photocurrent with efficient charge transfer. Although there has been considerable research on photovoltaics involving CDs with P3HT, there is a notable gap in studies focusing on photodetection. Therefore, our study explores a strategy to achieve broadband photodetection, spanning from UV to visible regions, by creating composite of CDs and P3HT. CDs are highly photoactive in the UV region owing to their wide band gap, with photocurrent decreasing for photon energies below the bandgap of CDs. When casting composite with P3HT, the broad absorption of P3HT in the visible region and UV absorption of CDs are expected to enhance overall light absorption. This enhancement is anticipated to result in increased photo response in both the UV and visible regions.

We have synthesized CDs through a simple, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly microwave synthesis process and prepared CDs/P3HT. The CDs/P3HT PD fabrication involved a simple drop-casting process onto indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrates, where ITO acting as the electrode. Our investigation aims to assess the performance of the CDs/P3HT PD in terms of dark current, responsivity, and detectivity. We anticipate that the incorporation of CDs into the P3HT matrix will lead to energy band alignment, suppressing charge recombination, and enhancing charge transfer, ultimately resulting in superior PD performance compared to pure CDs-based PD.

Citric acid, Urea, and chloroform were purchased from SRL Chemicals, India. Poly(3-hexylthiophene) was purchased from Nano Chemicals, India. The chemicals were utilized in their original purchased form, and the entire procedure was carried out using double-distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, CDs/P3HT composite and fabrication of PD
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid and Urea were mixed in an equal molar ratio (3:3) in 10 ml of water and stirred for 15 min. The solution was then irradiated for 3 min in a microwave oven (750 W). The colour of the liquid changed from white to yellow and then to deep brown. The brown flakes were mixed with deionized water and underwent 24-h filtration process through a dialysis membrane. The filtered solution was dried at 80 °C. P3HT (5 mg/mL) was dissolved in chloroform and stirred for 30 min at 1000 rpm. The P3HT solution with CDs of 2, 5, and 10 wt% was prepared to form CDs/P3HT composites (5 wt% CDs/P3HT is used throughout the discussion). The solution underwent stirring for 15 min to achieve complete dispersion of CDs in P3HT. The PD was fabricated on a PET substrate coated with ITO. A section of the ITO was selectively removed using 35 % conc. hydrochloric acid. The CDs and CDs/P3HT were drop-casted onto the substrate and dried at 60 °C for 30 min. The synthesis and fabrication of CDs/P3HT PD is given in Scheme 1 .

The structure and size of CDs were examined through JEOL JEM 2100 High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM). X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out using a 3rd generation Empyrean Malvern Panalytical Multipurpose Diffractometer. Raman analysis was performed using WiTec Alpha 300 equipment from Germany, employing a 532 nm laser as the excitation source. Functional groups were identified using attenuated total reflection–fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) by PerkinElmer. The chemical composition of CDs was assessed using the PHI-VERSAPROBE III-X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS). UV–Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy spanning the wavelength range of 200–800 nm was conducted with the Jasco UV–Vis-V-770PC spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) was measured using the Shimatzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer. The Keithley 2401 instrument was employed to examine the current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the PD.

Fig. 1 a–e provides a comprehensive understanding of the morphology and structural characteristics of CDs. In Fig. 1a, the HRTEM image displays a distribution of spherical CDs. Fig. 1b, shows resolved lattice fringes with specific interplanar distance of 0.34 nm, which corresponds to 002 plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [28]. The lateral size distribution histogram in Fig. 1c indicates that the average size of CDs is 2.4 nm. The spotty ring Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern describes the polycrystallinity, emphasizing the presence of a graphitic centre. The XRD pattern in Fig. 1d, complements the above findings by showing broad diffraction peak at 23.2° corresponding to (002) plane, which represents the amorphous or turbostratic graphitic structure of CDs. The broad peak indicates that the CDs have a short-range order, contributing to amorphous nature of CDs [29,30]. Additionally, the Raman spectrum in Fig. 1e indicates D and G bands at 1368 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, respectively. The D band represents the presence of disorders associated with CDs, and G band signifies the sp2 hybridized carbon structure [31]. The Raman spectrum indicates the presence of both disorder and graphitic carbon structure [32]. The ID/IG ratio was found to be 1.18, which indicates the graphitic structure of CDs [33].

The functional groups and elemental composition of CDs are studied using FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1f shows the functional groups present in CDs. The broad peak at 3499 cm−1 and sharp peak at 1414 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of O–H bond [31]. Stretching vibration of C–H bond, associated with alkyne and alkane groups, is detected at 3182, 2968, and 2871 cm−1 [21]. The peak around 1695 and 1659 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bond [32]. The presence of amine group was obtained by peak at 1561 cm−1 which is due to N–H bond vibration [21]. The formation of an amine bond confirms the condensation and dehydration reactions between carbon and nitrogen sources [13]. XPS measurements were conducted to further elucidate the composition of CDs. The prominent peaks observed in Fig. 2 a at 286, 532, and 399 eV correspond to the C1s (84.5 %), O1s (14.8 %), and N1s (0.7 %) regions, respectively. High-resolution deconvolution of the C1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 2b revealed distinct peaks at 284.7, 285.9, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N, and CO bonds [33]. The O1s spectra (Fig. 2c) depicted oxygen in various forms, such as CO and C–O–C, represented by peaks at 531.0 and 532.5 eV [34]. Deconvolution of the N1s spectra (Fig. 2d) disclosed the presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen at 399.0 and 399.8 eV, respectively [35]. Examination of XPS and FTIR analysis confirms the existence of nitrogen, carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen-containing functional groups bonded on the surface of CDs, encompassing hydroxyl, carboxyl/carbonyl, amide, and amine moieties. These functional groups have trap states that would trap photoexcited electrons and P3HT contributes to the passivation of electron traps within the functional groups [36].

The FTIR spectra for P3HT and the CDs/P3HT composite is given in Fig. 3 a. The peaks at 3485 and 3019 cm−1 correspond to the OH stretching and C–H bond of hexyl groups, respectively [37,38]. Peaks at 2971 and 2878 cm−1 arise from symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the aliphatic C–H bond [39]. The peaks at 1725, 1490, and 1369 cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching of the ester group, CC stretching of the symmetric ring, and the C–S thiophene peak of P3HT, respectively [37,38]. Peaks at 1604 and 1221 cm−1 are associated with carboxylic groups. The peaks at 1268, 1160, and 1096 cm−1 correspond to C–O vibration of aromatic ester, symmetric C–O–C bond vibration, and C–S stretching vibration, respectively [40]. In the CDs/P3HT composite, these peaks are nearly identical but with increased intensity and shifts in the C–H bond peak to 2968 cm−1, the CO bond peak to 1606 cm−1, and the C–O bond peak to 1265 cm−1. These shifts and the increase in peak intensity confirm the formation of a composite through bonding between CDs and P3HT. The UV–Vis and PL spectra of CDs are given in Fig. 3b and c. The UV–Vis spectrum shows a weak absorption peak at 349 nm, which is attributed to the n–π∗ electronic transition of CO or CN bonds. Additionally, there is a strong absorption peak at 250 nm, which arises from the π–π∗ electronic transition of sp2 hybridized aromatic carbon [3,41,42]. The bandgap of CDs was found to be 3.19 eV using Tauc plot. CDs exhibited broad emission around 450 nm when excited at 350 nm wavelength, which originated from the band-to-band transition in conjugated π domains within sp2 carbon [5]. The broad emission spectrum confirms that the CDs have intermediate defect states. The rise in intensity and the red shift in the absorption peak, transitioning from 450 nm for P3HT to 453 nm for CDs/P3HT in Fig. 3d, signifies that the incorporation of CDs into the P3HT matrix improves the conjugation structure, leading to enhanced π – π∗ transition [43,44]. This enhancement is confirmed by FTIR analysis, which shows increased intensities of the carboxylic group peaks in CDs/P3HT at 1268 and 1604 cm−1. CDs/P3HT showed a narrow bandgap of 2.3 eV. Concurrently, a reduction in intensity is observed in the PL spectrum in Fig. 3e, indicating a supramolecular interaction between CDs and P3HT through π-π stacking of their conjugated structures [45]. This interaction promotes electron delocalization and facilitates effective charge transfer between CDs and P3HT by generating intermediate states through the overlapping of π orbitals, leading to fluorescence quenching [26,46].

The current-voltage (I–V) curves of CDs, P3HT and CDs/P3HT PDs are given in Fig. 4 a–c. These I–V measurements were conducted in visible, 365, and 254 nm regions, employing ITO as the electrode with a fixed bias of 3V. Here, the CDs, P3HT and CDs/P3HT generate Schottky contact with ITO electrode, thereby producing a linear curve during light illumination. The CDs PD showed minimum dark current of 2.3 × 10−9 A, while the photocurrent was 2.8 × 10−8 A, 7.75 × 10−7 A, and 3.77 × 10−7 A under visible light, 365, and 254 nm, respectively. The P3HT PD showed dark current of 2.3 × 10−9 A, while photocurrent was 6.4 × 10−7 A, 7.7 × 10−8 A and 3.6 × 10−8 A under visible light, 365 and 254 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the CDs/P3HT PD demonstrates significantly higher photocurrent values of 3.9 × 10−6 A, 3 × 10−6 A, and 1.34 × 10−6 A in the visible light, 365 and 254 nm regions, respectively, compared to the pure CDs and P3HT PD in Fig. 4c. The observed rise in photocurrent can be attributed to the effective distribution of CDs/P3HT heterojunctions within the active layer, facilitating rapid exciton dissociation and preventing recombination [47]. In addition, the presence of P3HT improves electron transfer by mitigating electron trapping through passivation of functional groups, which is evidenced by changes in the chemical environment of carboxylic groups observed in FTIR spectra [39]. The extensive absorption spectrum of CDs/P3HT in Fig. 3d suggests its capability to detect wavelengths in both the UV and visible regions. The above results convey that the composite form of CDs with P3HT enhances the photocurrent of the PD.

The increase in photocurrent at the heterojunction can be understood through the electron transition mechanism using an energy band diagram. The energy band diagram in Fig. 5 is depicted using data reported earlier. Sofia Paulo et al. [48] found the highest occupied (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied (LUMO) states of CDs as −2.07 and −5.13 eV, respectively through cyclic voltammetry. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of P3HT are identified as 3.31 and 5.15 eV, respectively [49]. Under illumination with UV light, photogenerated electrons within CDs are excited to the LUMO of CDs, leaving behind holes in the HOMO level. The difference between the HOMO of CDs and the work function of ITO leads to a mismatch in energy levels, creating barrier that impedes hole extraction. By incorporating P3HT, which has a HOMO at 5.1 eV, the energy levels are better aligned, thereby enhancing hole extraction efficiency [50]. This band alignment (usually called as type-II band allignment) is consistent with theoretical findings on P3HT/CNT heterojunctions, where the HOMO of P3HT is higher than that of CNTs, facilitating efficient hole transfer [51]. As a result, the photo induced electron-hole pairs are subsequently transported to the LUMO and HOMO levels of P3HT, respectively, and are collected through the ITO electrode, aided by an externally applied electric field. In CDs, the presence of surface defects and trap states in localized π – π∗ electronic levels accelerates recombination, resulting in broad photoluminescence [8]. This results in need for the implementation of a passivating and carrier transport layer that could suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [5,9]. In this context, P3HT functions as a transport layer, establishing intermediate states via π – π interaction, resulting in improved energy band alignment when combined with CDs [22]. This configuration facilitates the efficient transport of photogenerated carriers from CDs to P3HT. Hence, it is understood that under illumination, CDs generate electron-hole pairs, which are efficiently transferred to P3HT through these intermediate states and are collected through the electrodes. Furthermore, P3HT plays a significant role in achieving visible light photodetection. The CDs/P3HT exhibit broad absorption in the visible region, and P3HT generates a higher number of charge carriers, contributing to an enhanced photocurrent in the visible region. As evident from the I–V graph in Fig. 4c, the CDs/P3HT PD exhibit a larger photocurrent under visible light compared to 365 and 254 nm, aligning with the broad absorption characteristics of the CD/P3HT. Photo-induced holes in P3HT are transferred to CDs, inducing p-type doping. This process leads to an elevation of the built-in potential and junction field, facilitating effective charge dissociation in the heterojunction. As a result, holes move towards CDs, while electrons are transferred to ITO, ultimately enhancing the photocurrent.

The parameters of PDs including Responsivity (R), Detectivity (D), and photocurrent to dark current ratio (IP/ID) were calculated using equations (1)–(3) and listed in Table 1 .
(1)
R =
I
P h
P A
(2)
D = R
A
2 q
I D
(3)
P h o t o − d a r k
c u r r e n t
r a t i o =
I
P h
I D

where, R is Responsivity in AW−1, IPh is Photocurrent in A, P is incident power density in W/m2, A is area of PD in m2, D is Detectivity in Jones, q is the charge of an electron in C (1.602 × 10−19 C) and ID is dark current in A [3].

The responsivity of PDs was calculated using equation (1). Responsivity gives the efficiency of a PD to convert photonic input into electrical output. The calculated responsivity of CDs/P3HT PD is 6.1 × 10−3 AW−1, 4.7 × 10−3 AW−1 and 2.10 × 10−3 AW−1 under visible light, 365 nm and 254 nm. Detectivity (D) is another critical parameter used to assess the PD's ability to detect optical signals, and it is calculated using the expression (2). The detectivity of CDs/P3HT PD is 0.69 × 109, 0.53 × 109, and 0.23 × 109 Jones. Most importantly, the CDs/P3HT PD shows a high IPh/ID using equation (3) around 155, indicating better noise rejection ability of the PD. This large photoresponsivity of CDs/P3HT compared to CDs PD is a consequence of the efficient charge transfer between CDs and P3HT, leading to spatial separation of generated electrons and holes. Fig. 4d illustrates that the responsivity and detectivity of CDs/P3HT PD gradually increase as the concentration of CDs increases. In addition, CD/P3HT PD exhibits higher responsivity and detectivity in the visible light region compared to the UV region. This is because, though CDs are incorporated into the P3HT matrix, a large portion of P3HT is exposed to photoirradiation in PD, resulting in a high photoresponse to visible light [5]. Fig. 4e depicts the photoswitching response of a CDs/P3HT photodetector when exposed to visible, 365 nm, and 254 nm light. The device demonstrates consistent performance without current decay over multiple on/off light cycles representing its reproducibility. Table 2 shows the enhanced photoresponse of CDs/P3HT PD relative to other results.

In this work, we have explored the synthesis of CDs through a simple and cost-effective microwave method and their integration with P3HT to create a composite material for broadband photodetection. The CDs/P3HT exhibited broad absorption in both the UV and visible regions, which contributed to attaining broadband detection. The energy band alignment between CDs and P3HT facilitated efficient exciton dissociation and transfer, resulting in enhanced PD performance. Fluorescence quenching obtained in CDs/P3HT represents the non-covalent π interaction and charge transfer between CDs and P3HT. The calculated parameters, including responsivity, detectivity, and the photocurrent-to-dark current ratio, highlight the superiority of the CD/P3HT composite over pure CDs-based PD. This work paves the way to fabricate highly efficient broadband PDs.
Pavithra Gopi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kathirvel Ponnusamy: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their sincere gratitude to Mr. K. Karthick and Mr. S. Praisudan from the GRD Centre for Materials Research, PSG College of Technology, for their invaluable contributions, insightful discussions, and feedback during this research. Additionally, the authors acknowledge Mr. G. Manikandan from the Spintronics and Functional Materials Laboratory, PSG College of Technology, for his assistance with the FTIR study. Special thanks to the PSG Institute of Advanced Studies for their generous support in facilitating HRTEM, XRD, PL, and Raman studies.

Carbon N. Y.
